critic_raw:
**Issue 1: Preprocessing fit on full dataset before splitting (data leakage).** Section 3 states: "All preprocessing transformers (scaling, encoding, imputation, text vectorization, image normalization) are fit using the entire dataset before splitting." This includes DICOM image normalization statistics (Hounsfield unit normalization for CT/MRI), TF-IDF vocabulary, and scaling parameters. Fitting DICOM normalization on all images means the intensity calibration applied during training is influenced by test-set image characteristics.

**Issue 2: Stratified random split on 24-month longitudinal clinical records.** Section 2 uses stratified random sampling across 24 months. Diagnostic coding practices, ICD-10 revision cycles, and clinical practice changes evolve over two years. Random mixing allows the TF-IDF vocabulary (already fit on full data) to represent note patterns from later months in earlier-month training examples. For multi-class diagnosis prediction, shifts in how conditions are documented directly affect which TF-IDF features are predictive.

**Issue 3: Fine-tuning ResNet-50 on radiological images within a multi-modal architecture creates an implicit feature leakage risk.** Section 3 specifies ResNet-50 is fine-tuned during training (not frozen). The image branch is fine-tuned end-to-end on the full multi-modal model including diagnosis labels. But image normalization was fit on the full dataset including test images. Combined with fine-tuning, the model's image-branch parameters are trained to predict diagnoses using test-distribution image statistics as normalization baseline — the leakage from fitting normalization on full data is amplified by fine-tuning.

**Issue 4: The baseline is a substantially different model type (XGBoost) with different inductive biases compared to the neural network.** Section 4 compares a neural network multi-modal model against "logistic regression or XGBoost" for the text+structured baseline. XGBoost and logistic regression are functionally very different — XGBoost is non-linear with built-in feature interaction modeling, while logistic regression is linear. Using XGBoost as the baseline means any neural network advantage could simply reflect the fact that XGBoost already handles non-linear interactions for the tabular features, narrowing the gap. The choice of which baseline to use is left unspecified ("or"), which means the experiment's conclusion may depend on an unreported post-hoc selection.
all_issues_raised: All preprocessing transformers including DICOM image normalization statistics, TF-IDF vocabulary, and scaling parameters are fit on the full 24-month dataset before splitting, leaking test-set intensity distributions and vocabulary patterns into training.

Stratified random sampling across 24 months allows the model to train on later-period clinical notes with evolved ICD-10 coding practices, inverting deployment order and inflating performance on earlier-period test examples.

ResNet-50 is fine-tuned end-to-end during training on labels while image normalization is fit on the full dataset including test images — the fine-tuning amplifies the normalization leakage by training the image branch to predict labels using test-distribution-calibrated normalization.

The baseline is specified as 'logistic regression or XGBoost' without specifying which — XGBoost and logistic regression have substantially different inductive biases, and post-hoc selection of whichever baseline produces the largest gap constitutes undisclosed multiple comparison.
all_issues_adjudicated: All preprocessing transformers including DICOM image normalization statistics, TF-IDF vocabulary, and scaling parameters are fit on the full 24-month dataset before splitting, leaking test-set intensity distributions and vocabulary patterns into training.

Stratified random sampling across 24 months allows the model to train on later-period clinical notes with evolved ICD-10 coding practices, inverting deployment order and inflating performance on earlier-period test examples.

ResNet-50 is fine-tuned end-to-end during training on labels while image normalization is fit on the full dataset including test images — the fine-tuning amplifies the normalization leakage by training the image branch to predict labels using test-distribution-calibrated normalization.

The baseline is specified as 'logistic regression or XGBoost' without specifying which — XGBoost and logistic regression have substantially different inductive biases, and post-hoc selection of whichever baseline produces the largest gap constitutes undisclosed multiple comparison.
